question: Let (X, d) be a metric space and let A ⊆ X. Define the interior of A, denoted int(A) or A°. Use the definition of an open ball, B(x, r) = { y ∈ X | d(x, y) < r }, to formally state what it means for a point x to be an interior point of A.
expectedPoints: A point x ∈ A is an interior point of A if there exists some radius r > 0 such that the open ball centered at x with that radius is entirely contained within A.
Formally: ∃ r > 0 such that B(x, r) ⊆ A.
The interior of A is the set of all its interior points: int(A) = { x ∈ A | ∃ r > 0, B(x, r) ⊆ A }.

question: Prove that for any subset A of a metric space (X, d), the interior of A, int(A), is an open set. Explain why this is considered the largest open set contained within A.
expectedPoints: To prove int(A) is open, take any point x ∈ int(A). By definition, ∃ r > 0 such that B(x, r) ⊆ A.

Now, show that this ball B(x, r) is also contained within int(A). For any y ∈ B(x, r), find a new radius r' > 0 (e.g., using the triangle inequality) such that B(y, r') ⊆ B(x, r) ⊆ A. This proves y is also an interior point of A, so B(x, r) ⊆ int(A).
Since every point in int(A) has an open ball around it contained in int(A), int(A) is open.
It is the largest open set contained in A because any other open set U ⊆ A must consist entirely of interior points of A, hence U ⊆ int(A).

question: Consider the set A = { (x, y) ∈ ℝ² | x² + y² ≤ 1 } ∪ { (2, 0) } in the standard Euclidean metric space. Determine the interior of A, int(A). Justify your answer rigorously by considering the definition of an interior point and the properties of the set. Furthermore, discuss how the interior would change if the metric were changed to the discrete metric.
expectedPoints: In the Euclidean metric: The interior is the open unit disk, { (x, y) ∈ ℝ² | x² + y² < 1 }. Points on the boundary (x²+y²=1) are not interior because any ball around them will contain points outside A.

The isolated point (2, 0) is not an interior point because any ball around it, no matter how small, will contain only that single point, which is not an open set in the Euclidean topology (it does not contain an open ball around (2,0) itself).
Justification requires applying the definition for a point on the boundary and the isolated point.
In the discrete metric, where d(x, y) = { 0 if x=y, 1 if x≠y }, every point is isolated. The open ball B(x, 0.5) = {x} for any x.
Therefore, for any point x ∈ A, B(x, 0.5) = {x} ⊆ A. This means every point in A, including the boundary points of the disk and the point (2,0), is an interior point. Thus, int(A) = A.
